coredata.dc:title: Structural design and solid electrolyte interphase modulation of SiOC-based anodes via N-doping for fast-charging lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Juan
Li, Yaqian
Jin, Siwen
Zheng, Xiuhui
Yuan, Juncong
Kong, Debin
Hu, Han
Feng, Xiang
Chen, De
coredata.dc:description: Silicon oxycarbide (SiOC)-based anodes are promising for enhancing the energy density of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), but face challenges like slow kinetics and poorly stable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI). Here, we address these limitations through nitrogen doping to regulate the composition of free carbon and SEI in SiOC-based anode, thereby enhancing the reaction kinetics and stability. With combined experimental and theoretical studies, N-doped SiOC-based particles with engineered carbon defects effectively reduce diffusion barriers. Furthermore, N-doped SiOC-based material forms a Li3N/LiF-dominated SEI with high ionic conductivity and interface stability, leading to exceptional performance. Consequently, N-doped SiOC-based anode exhibits a reversible capacity of 686.2 mAh g−1 with nearly 100 % retention over 1100 cycles at 1.0 A g−1 and stability for 2000 cycles at 2.0 A g−1. This work highlights the synergistic benefits of structural tuning and SEI modulation, paving the way for fast-charging LIBs and advanced energy storage systems.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Norway institutions: Core Hybrid journals RAP 2025
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon defect
N-doping
SiOC-based anode
Li3N/LiF-SEI
Lithium ion batteries

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical kinetic analysis of SiOC and N/SiOC electrodes
Electrodes discussion for enhanced performance mechanisms

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation processes of N/SiOCs composites. XRD patterns (b), N2 adsorption and desorption isotherm curves (c) and Raman spectra (d) of the SiOC and N/SiOCs materials. EPR spectra of defective carbon (e), the high-resolution XPS spectra of Si 2p (f) and C 1 s (g) of SiOC and N/SiOC samples. SEM images of SiOC (h), N/SiOC (i) and 2 N/SiOC (j). EDS mapping images (k) of the N/SiOC.

Electrochemical performance characterizations: Typical discharge-charge profiles (a) and rate performance (b). (c) Performance comparison between N/SiOC and recently reported Si-based and SiOC-based anodes. (d) Cycling performance at 1 A g−1 of SiOC and N-doped SiOCs electrodes. (e) Cycling performance at 0.5 A g−1 of SiOC and N/SiOC electrodes. CE-cycle number plots of SiOC and N/SiOC electrodes: the first few cycling (f) and subsequent cycle (g).

Electrochemical kinetic analysis of SiOC and N/SiOC electrodes: Log(i) versus Log(v) at peak 1 (a) and peak 2 (b). (c) The percentages of pseudocapacitive contribution at different sweep rates of SiOC and N/SiOC. The CV curve at 1 mV s−1 with detailed pseudocapacitive contribution of SiOC (d) and N/SiOC (e). In situ EIS spectra of SiOC (f) and N/SiOC (g). (h) EIS patterns of SiOC and N/SiOC after 100 cycles. (i) EIS patterns of N/SiOC after different cycles.

(a-b) Li+ diffusion coefficients of SiOC and N/SiOC. (c) Charge density differences of a single Li atom absorbed in SiOC (amorphous carbon) and N/SiOC (amorphous carbon with N and defect). (d-e) DFT calculated energy barriers of Li+ diffusions in SiOC (amorphous carbon) and N/SiOC (amorphous carbon with N and defect).

Lithium-ion storage mechanism of SiOC and N/SiOC electrodes: Ex-situ XRD patterns of SiOC and N/SiOC at first reaction (a) lithiation and (b) delithiation. HRTEM images of the N/SiOC (c) and SiOC (d) after 300 cycles at 1 A g−1. Cross-sectional SEM images of SiOC (e, g) and N/SiOC (f, h) electrodes before cycling and after 300 cycles at 1 A g−1.

Lithium-ion storage mechanism of SiOC and N/SiOC electrodes: (a-c) Etching curves and peak area ratios of different binding energy peaks of Li 1 s. (d-f) Etching curves and peak area ratios of different binding energy peaks of C 1 s. (g) Schematic illustration of the SEI structure of N/SiOC and SiOC electrodes.

head.ce:title: Structural design and solid electrolyte interphase modulation of SiOC-based anodes via N-doping for fast-charging lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Norwegian University of Science and Technology, Trondheim 7491, Norway

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Norwegian University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Norwegian University of Science and Technology, Trondheim 7491, Norway

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silicon oxycarbide (SiOC)-based anodes are promising for enhancing the energy density of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), but face challenges like slow kinetics and poorly stable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI). Here, we address these limitations through nitrogen doping to regulate the composition of free carbon and SEI in SiOC-based anode, thereby enhancing the reaction kinetics and stability. With combined experimental and theoretical studies, N-doped SiOC-based particles with engineered carbon defects effectively reduce diffusion barriers. Furthermore, N-doped SiOC-based material forms a Li3N/LiF-dominated SEI with high ionic conductivity and interface stability, leading to exceptional performance. Consequently, N-doped SiOC-based anode exhibits a reversible capacity of 686.2 mAh g−1 with nearly 100 % retention over 1100 cycles at 1.0 A g−1 and stability for 2000 cycles at 2.0 A g−1. This work highlights the synergistic benefits of structural tuning and SEI modulation, paving the way for fast-charging LIBs and advanced energy storage systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have attracted extensive research and achieved commercial adoption in energy storage systems [1–4]. Nevertheless, the energy density of LIBs with a graphite anode is nearing its theoretical limit due to the low lithium storage capacity (372 mAh g−1) [5]. In response, the development of high-performance silicon-based anode materials has become the focus of researchers [6–8]. Among these, silicon oxycarbide (SiOC)-based materials have been identified as promising anodes for developing next-generation high-energy LIBs. This is attributed to their high specific capacity, tunable chemical composition, and facile fabrication process [9,10]. Nevertheless, there are still fundamental challenges retarding the practical application of SiOC-based materials, including low electrical conductivity and unstable solid electrolyte interphase (SEI), resulting in unsatisfactory rate capability and low coulombic efficiency (CE) [11–13].

To address these challenges, substantial efforts have been directed toward enhancing the structure of SiOC-based materials. SiOC-based composites comprise three primary components: a tetrahedral Si-O-C region (including carbon-rich and oxygen-rich units), nanovoids (micropores), and free carbon (amorphous carbon) regions [14,15], which play critical roles in charge storage [12]. Studies have demonstrated that increasing the number of carbon-rich units can improve the rate performance of SiOC-based materials, albeit at the cost of reduced capacity enhancement [16–18]. Notably, the presence of free carbon not only provides effective Li+ storage sites but also facilitates electron conduction pathways within the SiOC matrix [16,19,20]. For instance, Graczyk-Zajc et al. explored the influence of disorders and defects in free carbon and found that carbon defects significantly enhance the electrochemical performance of SiOC-based materials [21]. In parallel, extensive research has focused on improving the electrical conductivity of carbon through nitrogen doping, with a wide range of N-doped carbon materials utilized in the development of Si/C anodes [22–26]. However, there remains a significant gap in understanding the effects of nitrogen doping specifically within the free carbon regions of SiOC-based materials, highlighting a critical area for future investigation.

In addition to the structural modulation of the SiOC-based anode, the composition of the SEI film is equally crucial to mechanical and electrical properties. A few studies have attempted to structure a stable SEI within Si-based anodes to improve poor stability [27–31]. In particular, the design of a stable SEI with increased ion conductivity is essential to achieve superior fast charging capability for high energy density LIBs [32]. In contrast to organic SEI species, inorganic components typically predominate the properties of the SEI, encompassing aspects such as ionic conductivity, mechanical robustness, and chemical/electrochemical stability [33–35]. Therein, LiF plays as a pivotal role in the SEI due to its low Li+ diffusion barrier, high electrical insulation, and robust mechanical strength [32,36–38]. Currently, the design of SEI predominantly focuses on Si-based anodes, while the performance of SiOC-based anodes is not always satisfactory. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a novel strategy that effectively modifies the carbon structure while forming a high ion conductivity and robust SEI in SiOC-based materials.

In this work, we introduce a novel approach to construct a SiOC-based material (N/SiOC) with carbon defects and N-doping, utilizing urea as the nitrogen source. During the lithiation reaction, lithium interacts with nitrogen atom in amorphous carbon to form the Li3N/LiF-dominated SEI film, which has excellent ionic conductivity and robust mechanical strength. Cyclic voltammograms (CV) analysis, galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT), and density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out to elucidate the ultrafast lithium-ion diffusion kinetics. Our results underscore the synergistic impact of carbon defects, N-doping, and the Li3N/LiF-dominated SEI in enhancing both the reaction kinetics and stability of the N/SiOC material. These ensure a high-rate capability and long-term stable cycling of the SiOC-based anode in lithium-ion storage. As a result, the optimized N/SiOC electrode delivers a capacity of 686.2 mAh g−1 after 1100 cycles at 1 A g−1 and retains nearly 100 % capacity retention after 2000 cycles at 2 A g−1. This work presents a strategy for the construction of high-rate and stable SiOC-based anodes with carbon defects and Li3N/LiF-SEI, offering new insights into the study of SiOC-based materials.

The fabrication of the N/SiOCs composites is depicted in Fig. 1(a). The SiOC-based composites used VTES as a precursor. Specifically, unlike SiOC, N-doped SiOCs (N/SiOC and 2 N/SiOC) are prepared with the addition of urea as a nitrogen source. Where the ratio of urea added in N/SiOC and 2 N/SiOC was 1 and 2, respectively. First, from the XRD pattern (Fig. 1b), both materials showed a broad peak at about 21°, which can be assigned to the amorphous SiOC phase [39]. In addition, the Li2Si2O5 (JCPDS 70–4856) and Li2SiO3 (JCPDS 29–0828) phases are present in SiOC and N-doped SiOCs materials [40], suggesting the presence of lithium silicate in both materials. This presence of lithium silicate is beneficial in reducing side reactions during the initial lithiation process. Furthermore, the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. 1c and Table S1) show that the specific surface areas of SiOC, N/SiOC and 2 N/SiOC are 4.78, 6.37 and 6.57 m2 g−1, respectively. The relatively small specific surface areas of the SiOC-based composites result in reduced electrolyte consumption, which improves the ICE. Notably, the pore volume and nano-voids of N/SiOC and 2 N/SiOC exceed those of SiOC (Fig. S1a and Table S1), suggesting that the porous structure is improved after N-doping.

Raman peaks at 1325 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 collected from SiOC and N-doped SiOCs (Fig. 1d) represent amorphous carbon and graphitized carbon, respectively [41]. Nevertheless, the I
D
/I
G ratio of N/SiOC (1.35) and 2 N/SiOC (1.34) is greater than that of SiOC (1.0), indicating that there are more defects in N-doped SiOCs due to the introduction of nitrogen. Deconvolution of the Raman spectrum also reveals T (~1145 cm−1) and D″ (~1520 cm−1) peaks, due to the existence of sp2-sp3-C bonds and the amount of amorphous carbon, respectively [9]. The intensity of T and D″ increases with N doping (Fig. 1d), further indicating an enhancement in the number of carbon defects in N-doped SiOCs samples [15]. Furthermore, the size (Lα) of carbon clusters can be determined from the intensity ratio (I
D
/I
G ) and Eq. (1). as suggested by Ferrari and Robertson [42].
(1)
I D
/
I G
=
C ’
λ
La 2

In Eq. (1), C′ represents a coefficient dependent on the laser's excitation wavelength, while Lα signifies the size of the carbon clusters. Since the I
D
/I
G value of N-doped SiOCs is higher than that of SiOC sample, the size of carbon clusters (Lα) increases with N doping according to formula 1. Consequently, these results suggest that nitrogen incorporation promotes the growth of carbon clusters and carbon defects within the SiOC-based sample. Furthermore, N-doping had little effect on the carbon content (Fig. S1b), with SiOC-based materials showing a carbon content of approximately 19.0 %. Meanwhile, the minimal differences in both nitrogen content and material properties between the N/SiOC and 2 N/SiOC samples suggest that urea-mediated nitrogen doping reaches saturation under these synthesis conditions. This indicates that simply increasing the concentration of urea may not be an effective strategy for further nitrogen incorporation in this system. Therefore, a systematic comparative analysis between N/SiOC and SiOC will be performed to elucidate the specific impacts of nitrogen doping.

The effect of N-doping on the degree of carbon defects and chemical composition was further analyzed using electron-paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Primarily, the defects in the carbon substrate can induce magnetism in these materials, resulting in a distinct peak in the EPR spectrum (Fig. 1e). The intensity of the EPR signal for the two materials at g = 2.0039 shows a defect degree order of N/SiOC>SiOC, implying that N doping promotes the formation of carbon defects. Subsequently, as exhibited in Fig. S1(c) and Table S2, XPS analysis revealed that all samples predominantly consist of the elements Si, O, C, N and Li. However, the N and C contents in N/SiOCs are markedly higher than those in SiOC, proving the successful doping of N by urea. Consequently, the electron conduction of N/SiOC surpasses that of SiOC due to the incorporation of nitrogen, which amplifies the electrical conductivity of carbon [43]. The XPS spectrum of Si 2p (Fig. 1f) consists of three peaks at 102.2, 103.1, and 104.0 eV, corresponding to the peaks of Si2+, Si3+, and Si4+, respectively [44]. The average valence states of silicon analyzed by Si 2p (Table S3) are 2.88 (SiOC) and 2.81 (N/SiOC), separately, indicating that N doping induces the conversion of SiOx to lower valence states. As shown in the high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 1g), three peaks located at 284.6, 285.5, and 288.8 eV can be observed for bonds of sp2-C, sp3-C/C-N, and CO, respectively [43]. The CC peak indicates the presence of pyrolytic carbon in the SiOC and N/SiOC samples. Meanwhile, N/SiOC exhibits a higher sp3-C/C-N occupancy compared to pure SiOC (Table S4), as evidenced by the decrease in the sp2-C/sp3-C ratio from 9.60 (SiOC) to 6.48 (N/SiOC). This observation further corroborates that the incorporation of N amplifies the C defects in N/SiOC, which is consistent with the Raman results. Further analysis of the N 1 s (Fig. S1d, Table S5) indicates that the proportion of nitrogen species varies between the two materials, which will consequently affect their properties. These results demonstrate the successful nitridation of the N/SiOC sample after the addition of urea, while the N doping changed the composition and increased its carbon defects.

SEM images (Fig. 1h-1j, Fig. S2) show that SiOC and N/SiOCs exhibit nano-stacked amorphous micrometer particles, which can display robust anti-fracture capability and provide additional space for expansion [45]. Meanwhile, the N/SiOCs and SiOC particles maintained above 100 nm, effectively precluding fragmentation mechanisms in ultrafine particle systems. In addition, the morphology of N/SiOCs is similar to that of SiOC, suggesting that N-doping does not alter the structure of SiOC-based materials. The morphology of N/SiOC is further verified by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The results show that N/SiOC is composed of nano-stacked amorphous micrometer particles (Fig. S3a-S3b). Moreover, the HAADF-STEM images and EDS mapping (Fig. S3c-f and Fig. 1k) reveal a uniform distribution of Si, O, and C, which promotes the multipoint contact of Si, thereby mitigating its volume expansion. Meanwhile, Fig. S3(d-f) shows that N/SiOC contains a certain amount of N and the N/C ratio in the outer layer is elevated. This further suggests that the inclusion of urea has altered the elemental composition of the SiOC-based material.

To evaluate the lithium-ion storage capabilities of SiOC-based anodes, a series of electrochemical measurements were conducted using half-cells with metallic lithium foil as the counter/reference electrode. Fig. 2(a) shows the first charge/discharge curves of the SiOC, N/SiOC and 2 N/SiOC electrodes at a current density of 0.1 A g−1. The N/SiOC and 2 N/SiOC provide a charge capacity of 1057.1 mAh g−1 with an ICE of 75.0 % and a charge capacity of 1033.8 mAh g−1 with an ICE of 74.3 %, respectively, both surpassing those of SiOC (933.8 mAh g−1 with a CE of 71.4 %). This phenomenon can be attributed to the presence of carbon defects in N/SiOC, which enhance the number of available lithium storage sites. Meanwhile, the reduced O/Si ratio in N/SiOC mitigates undesirable side reactions during the initial lithiation process. The rate performance of the three electrodes was tested at varying current densities (0.1–2.0 A g−1), as illustrated in Fig. 2b. The N-doped SiOC electrodes demonstrated superior rate capability, retaining 65 % of their capacity at 1 A g−1, significantly higher than the 54 % capacity retention of the undoped SiOC electrodes. This suggests that the carbon defects and the presence of N element in N-doped SiOCs can effectively improve electron transport and conductivity during the lithiation process. Fig. 2(d) displays the cycling stability performance of the electrodes at a high current density of 1.0 A g−1 after multiple cycles of electrochemical activation at 100 mA g−1. The SiOC-based samples exhibit a characteristic capacity evolution profile consisting of an initial activation phase, attributed to the progressive utilization of lithium silicate phases. The subsequent stage showed a slight decline in capacity, possibly due to structural deterioration [10,46]. Notably, the capacity of N-doped SiOCs electrodes at the 1100th cycle was about 686.2 mAh g−1, which was significantly higher than that of SiOC (431.1 mAh g−1). When comparing N/SiOC and 2 N/SiOC, the rate and cycling performance are essentially identical. This observation further confirms that the effects of nitrogen doping reach saturation in this synthetic system. Therefore, this study focuses on examining and analyzing the differences between N/SiOC and SiOC to better understand the impact of nitrogen doping. Especially, after rate capability testing, when the current density was returned to 0.5 A g−1, the N/SiOC electrode maintained a reversible capacity of 963.4 mAh g−1 after 800 cycles, significantly higher than that of pristine SiOC (Fig. 2e). Impressively, the high-capacity retention of approximately 95.3 % (relative to 30cy) was observed after 2000 cycles at 2.0 A g−1 (Fig. S4), suggesting the excellent durability of N/SiOC for LIBs.

To explain the excellent stability of N/SiOC with higher capacity than SiOC, the CE is shown in Fig. 2(f). The N/SiOC has a higher CE than SiOC at both high and low currents, indicating that the side reaction between the electrolyte and active material during cycling is mitigated in N/SiOC. Meanwhile, the CE of the N/SiOC quickly reached 99.8 % after merely 15 cycles and consistently surpassed that of SiOC in the following cycles (Fig. 2g), further suggesting that N doping induces the formation of the robust SEI. The presence of stable SEI in N/SiOC is further validated by the initial charge/discharge curves and CV under high current conditions. As illustrated in Fig. S5(a-b), the decomposition voltage of N/SiOC surpasses that of SiOC. This suggests that N doping enhances the decomposition voltage of FEC in N/SiOC, which can induce a stable inorganic SEI film at the beginning [36]. Furthermore, the CV curves at 0.1 mV s−1 for SiOC and N/SiOC are shown in Fig. S6. The overlap of N/SiOC is significantly greater than that of SiOC, suggesting better CE and stability of N/SiOC, consistent with the results in Fig. 2(f-g). Consequently, these results suggest that the parasitic reaction between the active material and the electrolyte during cycling is mitigated by the formation of robust and durable SEI in N/SiOC. Obviously, N/SiOC exhibits a high capacity retention ratio and excellent stability under large current when compared to the recently reported Si-based and SiOC-based materials (Fig. 2c) [9,10,13,36,47–49]. Given the remarkable performance of the N/SiOC anode, its potential for practical application in full cells LIBs was evaluated. Full cells comprising the NCM811 cathode and N/SiOC anode were assembled to assess practical feasibility (Fig. S7). Notably, the N/SiOC//NCM811 full cell demonstrates exceptional rate capability, maintaining a specific capacity of 144.2 mAh g−1 after 25 cycles at a high current density of 1.0C (1C = 200 mAh g−1). These results demonstrate the promising commercial potential of N/SiOC for lithium-ion battery applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical kinetic analysis of SiOC and N/SiOC electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
The reaction kinetics of SiOC and N/SiOC electrodes were investigated by the cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Primarily, the CV curves were obtained at various scan rates from 0.01 to 1 mV s−1. The peak current (i) increases with the scan rate (v), according to the power-law Eq. (2–3), as shown in Fig. S8(a-b).
(2)
i =
av b
(3)
log
i
= blog
v
+ log
a

The a and b are empirical constants, among which the b-value can identify the Li+ diffusion-dominated behavior (b = 0.5) and pseudocapacitive-dominated process (b = 1) [49]. The b-values for peak 1 and peak 2 are 0.71 and 0.76 for SiOC, 0.74 and 0.78 for N/SiOC, respectively (Fig. 3a-3b). These results indicate that both SiOC and N/SiOC exhibit predominant capacitive behavior in Li-ion storage. The contribution of this capacitive behavior can be derived from Eq. (4) and (5) [50],
(4)
i
V
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2
(5)
i
V
/
v
1 / 2
=
k 1
v
1 / 2
+
k 2

where k
1
v and k
2
v
1/2 indicate the pseudocapacitive-controlled fraction and diffusion-controlled fraction, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the capacitance control of N/SiOC becomes more pronounced with increasing scan rate and is consistently larger than that of SiOC. Apparently, the capacitance contribution at 1 mV s−1 of N/SiOC (74 %) exceeds that of SiOC (71 %), indicating greater ion storage and faster ion diffusion in N/SiOC (Fig. 3d and e). This enhancement is primarily due to the presence of carbon defects and nitrogen dopants. These modifications not only provide additional active sites for Li+ storage but also facilitate faster Li+ transport pathways, thereby significantly improving both the capacity and rate capability of the electrode.

Furthermore, in-situ EIS measurements were performed, with the results presented in Fig. 3(f-g) and Fig. S9. Compared to SiOC, the N/SiOC electrode exhibits a reduced Rct (charge-transfer resistance) during the charge-discharge process, reflecting its excellent electrical conductivity. Notably, at the initial lithiation stage of 1.1 V, the impedance of N/SiOC (Rct = 52.1 Ω) was significantly smaller than that of SiOC (Rct = 92.3 Ω) from the equivalent circuit fitting results (Fig. S10 and Table S6). Moreover, the electrolyte decomposition reaction had taken place at around 1.1 V (Fig. S5), and inorganic SEI films with lower EIS [34]. Thus, this implies that N/SiOC preferentially forms inorganic SEI films. Upon subsequent lithiation to 0.01 V, the Rct of N/SiOC remained consistently lower than those of SiOC, demonstrating that thinner and more inorganic-rich SEI is formed in N/SiOC than in SiOC. Concurrently, the transfer of Li+ in the active material can be assessed through Warburg impedance (the slope of the oblique line in the low-frequency region), where a higher slope indicates faster Li+ diffusion. Comparative analysis shows that the Warburg impedance of N/SiOC is consistently lower than that of SiOC (Fig. S10 and Fig. S11). These results indicate that Li+ diffusion impedance is smaller in N/SiOC than in SiOC, proving that the carbon defects and N-doping facilitate ion diffusion. Further analysis of the EIS for 50 and 100 cycles (Fig. 3h and Fig. S12) reveals that the impedance of N/SiOC is significantly lower than that of SiOC, indicating that the SEI formed by N/SiOC is thinner and more conductive than that of SiOC. Remarkably, Fig. 3(i) shows that the impedance of N/SiOC is almost unchanged after 50 and 100 cycles, indicating excellent stability. Therefore, these results imply that N/SiOC exhibits high reaction kinetics and stability in lithium storage compared to SiOC due to the presence of carbon defects and the formation of thinner and more stable SEI.

The galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) was used to further characterize the kinetics. From Fig. 4 (a-b) and Fig. S13, the DLi+ value derived from GITT for N/SiOC was significantly larger than that of SiOC, further corroborating its superior Li+ transport efficiency. To obtain a deeper understanding of the experimental results, first-principles calculations were conducted to further explore the influence of N and defect sites. Fig. 4(c) illustrates that the adsorption energies ΔE of the N/SiOC (amorphous carbon) and N/SiOC (amorphous carbon with N and defect) are −2.262 eV and − 3.301 eV, respectively. Thus, the N-doped substrate is more favorable for Li+-adsorption. Besides, the N-doped structure on the diffusion barrier of lithium ion, the CI-NEB method based on DFT calculations is conducted. As illustrated in Fig. 4 (d-e), N/SiOC exhibits a significantly lower energy barrier (1.2642 eV) than SiOC (1.5998 eV), demonstrating that the synergistic effect between nitrogen atoms and carbon defects facilitates Li+ diffusion. These advantages stem from two synergistic mechanisms: (1) nitrogen doping generates electron-rich domains that simultaneously reduce Li+ binding energy and create favorable adsorption sites, while (2) the accompanying carbon defect formation enables low-energy diffusion channels for enhanced Li+ mobility. Therefore, the theoretical calculations indicate that the N-doping carbon matrix, characterized by an increase in defects and nitrogen concentration, has a positive effect on the Li+-adsorption and diffusion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodes discussion for enhanced performance mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the high reaction kinetics and stability of N/SiOC in lithium storage relative to SiOC, ex-situ XRD was investigated for both electrodes discharged to 0.01 V and charged to 1.5 V. As shown in Fig. 5(a) and Fig. S14(a), more LixSi and LiC are generated in SiOC and N/SiOC electrodes as the Li uptake continues [51]. Notably, N/SiOC exhibits an additional peak at ~32°, corresponding to Li3N (JCPDS 78–2005) [52]. This observation confirms that nitrogen doping induces the formation of conductive Li3N in N/SiOC, which enhances lithium-ion transport. Subsequently, the crystalline LixSi and LiC transform into amorphous material during the charging process by recharging to 1.5 V (Fig. 5b and Fig. S14b). Based on the evidence presented, it can be concluded that SiOC-based anodes undergo a transition between crystalline and amorphous states during the process of lithium insertion and extraction.

The HRTEM analysis corroborates the presence of a Li3N/LiF-rich SEI layer in N/SiOC with a thickness of ~12 nm (Fig. 5c). The crystalline lattice fringes in the SEI layer were indexed to Li3N and LiF (Fig. S15), whereas the SEI in SiOC was thicker (~15 nm) and more amorphous (Fig. 5d). The thinner SEI in N/SiOC correlates with reduced electrode surface resistance, consistent with in-situ EIS results. To evaluate the structural stability, cross-sectional SEM images (Fig. 5e-5h) were analyzed before and after 300 charge/discharge cycles at 1 A g−1. The N/SiOC electrode exhibited smaller volume expansion (22.54 %) compared to SiOC (29.21 %). Meanwhile, the morphology and particle structure of N/SiOC remained largely unchanged after cycling, as observed in Fig. S16. This indicates that the carbon defects and Li3N/LiF-SEI in N/SiOC act as a buffer layer, preventing continuous SEI growth and minimizing volume expansion during the lithiation/delithiation process. Furthermore, the Li3N/LiF-rich SEI layer with high ionic conductivity and mechanical robustness, which effectively suppresses parasitic side reactions and mitigates self-discharge risks by stabilizing the electrode-electrolyte interface.

The argon ion etching XPS analysis provides further insight into the SEI composition of SiOC and N/SiOC electrodes after 300 cycles at 1 A g−1. As shown in Table S7-S8, the fluorine content in N/SiOC consistently exceeds that in SiOC, exhibiting an upward trend with increasing etch depth. The Li 1 s spectra (Fig. 6a-6c) reveal peaks for typical SEI components such as ROCO2Li (54.4 eV), Li2CO3 (55 eV), and LiF (55.5 eV). Among these, LiF plays a critical role due to its low lithium-ion diffusion barrier and high mechanical strength, which enhances mechanical stability and prevents excessive electrolyte consumption [53]. The intensity of the LiF peak in N/SiOC is consistently higher than that in SiOC, with a higher LiF fraction in the inner SEI (Fig. 6c). At an etching depth of 10 nm, the SiOC electrode retains significant signals for Li2CO3 and ROCO2Li, whereas N/SiOC exhibits a predominant LiF signal Quantitative analysis shows the LiF content in N/SiOC reaches 71.3 %, significantly higher than the 59.9 % observed in SiOC. Furthermore, the Li 1 s spectra of N/SiOC show a shift to higher binding energies with increasing etching depth (Fig. S17), indicating a higher proportion of LiF in the SEI.

The C 1 s spectra (Fig. 6d-6f) provide additional evidence for a thinner SEI film in N/SiOC. Peaks corresponding to CO3 2−, CO, CO, and CC at 289.8, 288.4, 286.4, and 284.8 eV, respectively, indicate SEI-related carbon species [54,55]. The lower relative intensities of CO and CO peaks in N/SiOC compared to SiOC suggest a thinner SEI film in N/SiOC. These findings demonstrate that nitrogen doping facilitates the formation of a Li3N/LiF-rich and thinner SEI in N/SiOC than that in SiOC (Fig. 6g), resulting in improved lithium-ion transport and superior cycling stability. Additionally, N/SiOC has demonstrated promising performance in sodium-ion batteries (Fig. S18 and S19), further underscoring its potential as an advanced anode material for next-generation metal-ion batteries.

In conclusion, this work highlights the critical role of free carbon structures in SiOC-based materials in influencing SEI formation and optimizing electrochemical performance for high-rate anode applications. By introducing nitrogen doping and engineering carbon defects, a robust Li3N/LiF-dominant SEI was successfully formed on the N/SiOC-based anodes. This structural modification enhanced lithium-ion transport kinetics, reduced electrode impedance, and significantly improved the stability of the SEI during repeated charge/discharge cycles. As a result, the N/SiOC composites demonstrated exceptional electrochemical performance, delivering a reversible capacity of 686.2 mAh g−1 after 1100 cycles at 1 A g−1 and maintaining remarkable stability over 2000 cycles at 2 A g−1. This study provides valuable insights into the impact of nitrogen doping on SEI composition and carbon defect engineering, effectively addressing challenges in cycling stability, rate capability, and long-term durability for LIBs. Furthermore, this structure-tuning strategy offers a simple yet versatile approach to extend the application of SiOC-based materials to other energy storage systems, such as sodium-ion batteries, paving the way for their broader implementation in advanced fast-charging technologies.

Juan Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Yaqian Li: Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Siwen Jin: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xiuhui Zheng: Software, Methodology. Juncong Yuan: Software, Methodology. Debin Kong: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Han Hu: Validation, Software. Xiang Feng: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. De Chen: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China-Outstanding Youth Foundation (22322814), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172040), and the Taishan Scholar Project of Shandong Province (No. tsqnz20221118).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.164661.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Capacity fade and its mitigation in Li-ion cells with silicon-graphite electrodes
